content.method: Structured comparative case synthesis (process tracing) of Finnish 1970s reform, Polish 1999 reform, and German partial detracking, coded for presence/absence/timing of complementary policies; capacity assessment of Hungarian teacher supply and fiscal space against the resource thresholds identified in these cases; expert elicitation with Hungarian education administrators on implementable subset.
content.question: Which specific complementary institutional features (funding equalization, teacher qualification requirements, curriculum mandates, catchment/nearby-school assignment defaults) are necessary conditions for structural tracking reform to yield measurable equity gains, and which of these can Hungary feasibly implement given current fiscal and teacher-supply constraints?
content.why_it_matters: The Finnish case shows structural reform alone was insufficient and took two decades with bundled complementary reforms; the Polish case shows transition costs and reversal risk are severe without careful sequencing. Any Hungarian reform proposal that treats structural change as a standalone lever risks replicating Finland's later erosion or Poland's implementation shocks.
